title: C# SOLID — Paper 8
description: Covers ISP refactoring, DIP concepts, DIP implementation, SRP concepts, SRP refactoring, Refactoring Techniques, Unit Testing & SOLID, SOLID Real C# Scenarios, Dependency Injection Patterns, and Strategy Pattern.

questions.text: ST12-7: A `SortContext` class holds a reference to an `ISortStrategy` interface. At runtime, the user selects QuickSort. The correct way to change strategy in C# is:

options.text: Instantiate a new SortContext with QuickSortStrategy.
Call `context.SetStrategy(new QuickSortStrategy())` or pass it via a property/method to swap strategies dynamically.
Subclass SortContext as QuickSortContext.
Use reflection to change the private strategy field.

questions.text: ST01-7: According to SRP, a class with a high 'lack of cohesion of methods' (LCOM) metric suggests:

options.text: The class has too few methods.
Methods in the class do not share instance variables or concerns, indicating the class likely has multiple responsibilities.
The class is well-designed and focused.
The class should be made abstract.

questions.text: ST19-12: Why does a class that uses `new ConcreteService()` internally tend to have poor unit test coverage?

options.text: The compiler refuses to compile it without a test.
The concrete instantiation cannot be replaced with a test double, so tests exercise the real dependency (e.g., a real database or network).
Tests cannot import classes that use `new`.
The DI container rejects it at startup.

questions.text: ST09-11: Which of the following is a violation of DIP in a repository pattern context?

options.text: An application service accepting ICustomerRepository in its constructor.
An application service directly referencing `EFCoreCustomerRepository` and calling `new EFCoreCustomerRepository(connectionString)` internally.
Registering EFCoreCustomerRepository as the implementation of ICustomerRepository in the DI container.
Defining ICustomerRepository in the domain layer.

questions.text: ST20-11: A `switch` statement in a method switches on an enum representing shape types to calculate area. A new shape is added and the developer must update the switch. Which principle is violated?

options.text: SRP — the method has too many responsibilities.
OCP — the method must be modified every time a new type is added.
DIP — the method depends on a concrete enum.
ISP — the method has too many parameters.

questions.text: ST08-12: In C#, how does the `IEnumerable<T>` (read-only iteration) vs `IList<T>` (read-write) distinction illustrate ISP?

options.text: Both expose the same surface; they are identical from an ISP perspective.
Methods that only need to iterate should accept IEnumerable<T>, not IList<T>, so callers are not forced to depend on mutation methods they don't use.
IList<T> should always be used because it is the richer interface.
IEnumerable<T> violates ISP because it has only one method.

questions.text: ST11-8: In Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection, a 'captive dependency' occurs when:

options.text: A singleton service depends on a transient service, potentially holding it alive for the application lifetime.
A transient service depends on another transient service.
Two services form a circular dependency.
A scoped service is not disposed at end of request.

questions.text: ST18-13: 'Rename Method' refactoring is primarily about:

options.text: Changing the method signature to accept different parameter types.
Improving code readability by giving a method a name that better communicates its intent.
Splitting a method into two methods with the original name retained.
Moving a method to a different class.

questions.text: ST02-7: A class `CustomerService` has three groups of private methods: one group for validation, one for CRUD, and one for notifications. The SRP-appropriate refactoring is to:

options.text: Keep all methods in CustomerService but prefix them by responsibility.
Extract CustomerValidator, CustomerRepository, and CustomerNotifier as separate classes and inject them into CustomerService.
Make all private methods public so they can be tested independently.
Create a single CustomerServiceHelper class for the private methods.

questions.text: ST10-8: Which of the following describes the key difference between DI via a container vs. manual DI (Poor Man's DI)?

options.text: Containers use reflection; manual DI uses source generators.
A DI container automates the construction and lifetime management of the object graph; manual DI requires wiring dependencies explicitly in code at the composition root.
Manual DI is always better because it avoids reflection overhead.
DI containers enforce SOLID principles; manual DI does not.

questions.text: ST19-13: When using Moq in C# to verify that a method was called, which syntax is correct?

options.text: mock.Assert(m => m.Send(It.IsAny<string>()))
mock.Verify(m => m.Send(It.IsAny<string>()), Times.Once())
mock.Expect(m => m.Send(It.IsAny<string>()))
mock.Check(m => m.Send(It.IsAny<string>()))

questions.text: ST20-12: A C# class `DataExporter` has methods `ExportToCsv()`, `ExportToJson()`, `ExportToXml()`. Each method is 80 lines long. Which SOLID refactoring applies?

options.text: Apply DIP — inject the format as an interface.
Apply OCP via Strategy — create ICsvExporter, IJsonExporter, IXmlExporter (or a single IExporter) and move logic into separate classes.
Apply ISP — split DataExporter into three interfaces.
Apply LSP — subclass DataExporter for each format.

questions.text: ST01-8: Which of the following class names suggests an SRP violation by naming convention alone?

options.text: OrderRepository
UserManagerAndEmailerAndLogger
InvoiceValidator
PaymentGateway

questions.text: ST12-8: The Strategy pattern aligns with SRP because:

options.text: Each strategy class has a single algorithm responsibility, keeping each class focused and testable.
The context class handles all algorithm selection logic.
Strategies eliminate the need for inheritance.
SRP and Strategy are unrelated.

questions.text: ST09-12: Which of the following correctly implements DIP with constructor injection in C#?

options.text: public class OrderService { private SqlRepository _repo = new SqlRepository(); }
public class OrderService { private readonly IOrderRepository _repo; public OrderService(IOrderRepository repo) { _repo = repo; } }
public class OrderService { public SqlRepository Repo { get; set; } }
public class OrderService { private IOrderRepository _repo => ServiceLocator.Get<IOrderRepository>(); }

questions.text: ST18-14: 'Replace Type Code with Subclasses' refactoring is appropriate when:

options.text: An enum has too many values.
A class uses an integer or enum field to switch behavior in many places; behavior differences can be encoded in subclasses or strategy implementations.
A type parameter is used only once.
A class inherits from too many base classes.

questions.text: ST02-8: 'Separation of concerns' as applied by SRP means:

options.text: Each class should be in a separate assembly.
Different aspects of a problem (e.g., business logic, persistence, UI) should be handled by separate classes, each responsible for its own concern.
A class should have only one method.
Concerns should be hidden behind abstract base classes only.

questions.text: ST11-9: In C#, which interface must a custom DI container's service provider implement to be compatible with ASP.NET Core?

options.text: IServiceFactory
IServiceProvider
IServiceCollection
IDependencyResolver

questions.text: ST20-13: Consider: `public void Save(object data) { if (data is Customer c) { SaveCustomer(c); } else if (data is Order o) { SaveOrder(o); } }`. What SOLID issue is present?

options.text: LSP — Save substitutes the wrong type.
OCP — the method must be modified every time a new data type needs saving; use IStorable interface or overloads instead.
ISP — the method accepts too broad a type.
DIP — object is a concrete type.

questions.text: ST19-14: 'Test isolation' means that a unit test should:

options.text: Only be run on an isolated machine.
Verify one behavior in isolation, using test doubles to remove all external dependencies so failures pinpoint the unit under test.
Avoid using mock objects.
Always test the entire system end to end.

questions.text: ST08-13: ISP and the Facade pattern relate in that:

options.text: Facade eliminates the need for ISP by exposing all methods through one object.
A Facade can present a simplified, role-specific interface to a client while hiding a complex subsystem, serving the same goal as ISP.
ISP is only applicable when a Facade is present.
Facade and ISP contradict each other.

questions.text: ST10-9: Which of the following correctly describes how to register an open generic type in Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection?

options.text: services.AddTransient(typeof(IRepository), typeof(EFRepository))
services.AddTransient(typeof(IRepository<>), typeof(EFRepository<>))
services.AddTransient<IRepository<>, EFRepository<>>()
services.AddGeneric<IRepository, EFRepository>()

questions.text: ST12-9: In C#, which of the following is a clean way to implement strategy selection based on runtime input?

options.text: A long if-else chain checking a string input for the strategy name.
A Dictionary<string, IStrategy> keyed by strategy name, returning the appropriate strategy instance for injection.
Reflection to find all types implementing IStrategy and instantiate them all.
A switch expression hardcoded to three strategy types.

questions.text: ST01-9: The SRP principle originated in which broader software engineering concept?

options.text: DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)
Cohesion — specifically the idea of high cohesion within a module, attributed to Larry Constantine and later formalized by Robert C. Martin.
YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It)
CAP theorem

questions.text: ST20-14: A developer adds logging to 15 different service classes by copying the same Logger.Log() call. Which code smell and SOLID violation does this represent?

options.text: Feature Envy and ISP.
Duplicate Code and OCP/SRP — logging should be extracted (e.g., as a Decorator or via AOP), not duplicated across classes.
Shotgun Surgery and DIP.
Long Method and LSP.

questions.text: ST18-15: 'Hide Delegate' refactoring is used to:

options.text: Make a delegate private.
Reduce message chains by having a class expose a method that wraps a call to a collaborator, hiding the delegate from the client.
Replace a delegate with an event.
Move a delegate field to a base class.

questions.text: ST09-13: The 'Stable Abstractions Principle' (related to DIP) states that:

options.text: A package should be as abstract as it is stable; highly stable packages should be highly abstract.
Abstract classes should never change once released.
All interfaces should be in the most stable assembly.
Abstractions should depend on concrete implementations for stability.

questions.text: ST02-9: A `ProductService` class contains: `CalculatePrice()`, `GenerateInvoice()`, `SendConfirmationEmail()`, `SaveToDatabase()`. After SRP refactoring, the correct architecture is:

options.text: Move all methods into a ProductHelper static class.
Extract PricingService, InvoiceService, NotificationService, and ProductRepository, then inject them into a thin orchestrating ProductService.
Keep ProductService but make all methods virtual for easy overriding.
Split ProductService into ProductServiceA and ProductServiceB arbitrarily.

questions.text: ST11-10: In C# ASP.NET Core, `IHttpContextAccessor` is typically registered as which lifetime, and why?

options.text: Transient — because HTTP context changes every millisecond.
Singleton — because the accessor is stateless; only the HttpContext it accesses is per-request.
Scoped — because it is tied to a specific HTTP request scope.
Transient — because each service needing it gets a fresh instance.

questions.text: ST19-15: What is 'test pyramid' and how does SOLID support it?

options.text: A pyramid-shaped class hierarchy used in test frameworks.

A model advocating many fast unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, and fewest UI/end-to-end tests at the top; SOLID supports this by making units small, isolated, and mockable.
A diagram showing code coverage targets.
A pyramid of abstract base classes used only in test projects.
